items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Pod-Level Resource Managers (Alpha)
items.summary:
<p>Kubernetes v1.36 introduces
<a href="https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/resource-managers/#pod-level-resource-managers">Pod-Level Resource Managers</a>
as an alpha feature, bringing a more flexible and powerful resource management
model to performance-sensitive workloads. This
items.reason: Kubernetes v1.36 introduces a brand‑new alpha feature (Pod‑Level Resource Managers) that is not covered by any existing wiki page. The feature is core to resource scheduling and will affect many workloads, so a dedicated article is required.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: In-Place Vertical Scaling for Pod-Level Resources Graduates to Beta
items.summary: <p>Following the graduation of Pod-Level Resources to Beta in v1.34 and the General Availability (GA) of In-Place Pod Vertical Scaling in v1.35, the Kubernetes community is thrilled to announce that <strong>In-Place Pod-Level Resources Vertical Scaling has graduated to Beta in v1.36!</stron
items.reason: In‑Place Vertical Scaling graduating to Beta is a major usability improvement for Kubernetes users. No current wiki entry exists, so a new document describing the feature, usage, and migration path is needed.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Tiered Memory Protection with Memory QoS
items.summary:
<p>On behalf of SIG Node, we are pleased to announce updates to the Memory QoS
feature (alpha) in Kubernetes v1.36. Memory QoS uses the cgroup v2 memory
controller to give the kernel better guidance on how to treat container memory.
It was first introduced in v1.22 and updated in v1.27. In Kubern
items.reason: Tiered Memory Protection with Memory QoS is a new alpha capability that impacts performance‑sensitive workloads. It warrants its own wiki page because it is not yet documented.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Staleness Mitigation and Observability for Controllers
items.summary:
<p>Staleness in Kubernetes controllers is a problem that affects many controllers, and is something may affect controller behavior
in subtle ways. It is usually not until it is too late, when a controller in production has already taken incorrect action, that
staleness is found to be an issue due
items.reason: Staleness mitigation and observability for controllers is a niche but important topic for cluster operators. No existing article covers it, so a new entry should be created.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Mutable Pod Resources for Suspended Jobs (beta)
items.summary:
<p>Kubernetes v1.36 promotes the ability to modify container resource requests and limits
in the pod template of a suspended Job to beta. First introduced as alpha in v1.35, this
feature allows queue controllers and cluster administrators to adjust CPU, memory, GPU,
and extended resource specific
items.reason: Mutable Pod Resources for suspended Jobs (beta) introduces a new workflow for job queues. This capability is not documented in the wiki, requiring a fresh article.

items.title: Precision Container Security with Docker and Black Duck
items.summary: The complexity of modern containerized applications often leaves developers drowning in a sea of "noise"—vulnerabilities that exist in the file system but pose zero actual risk to the application. The integration between Black Duck and Docker Hardened Images (DHI) provides a definitive answer to thi
items.reason: The integration of Docker Hardened Images with Black Duck addresses container supply‑chain security, a topic of high relevance to DevSecOps readers. No current wiki page covers this specific integration.

items.title: A Virtual Agent team at Docker: How the Coding Agent Sandboxes team uses a fleet of agents to ship faster
items.summary: I work on Coding Agent Sandboxes, aka “sbx” at Docker. The project provides secure, microVM-based isolation for running AI coding agents like Claude Code, Gemini, Codex, Docker Agent and Kiro. Agents get full autonomy inside a sandbox (their own Docker daemon, network, filesystem) without touching y
items.reason: Docker’s Virtual Agent team and its microVM‑based sandboxing for AI coding agents is a novel use‑case. While niche, it fits the wiki’s scope on container‑based isolation and therefore needs its own entry.

items.title: From Security Blocked to Prod Ready: ClickHouse on Docker Hardened Images
items.summary: In November 2025, a team self-hosting Langfuse, an open-source LLM observability platform, on Kubernetes uploaded their ClickHouse image to AWS ECR as part of their production preparation. They found that the pipeline scanner had returned three critical vulnerabilities - not in ClickHouse, but in th
items.reason: A case study of ClickHouse on Docker Hardened Images illustrates real‑world supply‑chain hardening. The wiki lacks such practical examples, so a new document is appropriate.

items.title: Trivy, KICS, and the shape of supply chain attacks so far in 2026
items.summary: Catching the KICS push: what happened, and the case for open, fast collaboration In the past few weeks we've worked through two supply chain compromises on Docker Hub with a similar shape: first Trivy, now Checkmarx KICS. In both cases, stolen publisher credentials were used to push malicious images
items.reason: Supply‑chain attacks involving Trivy and KICS in 2026 are highly topical for security practitioners. This warrants a comprehensive wiki article covering the incidents, mitigations, and lessons learned.

items.title: Register now for OpenClaw: After Hours @ GitHub
items.summary:
OpenClaw builders will gather at GitHub HQ during Microsoft Build 2026 for demos and conversations. Join in person, or watch the livestream on Twitch.
The post Register now for OpenClaw: After Hours @ GitHub appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: OpenClaw is an event tied to GitHub Build 2026. While less technical, it is still relevant for community‑focused sections of the wiki, justifying a brief event page.

items.title: GitHub Copilot CLI for Beginners: Interactive v. non-interactive mode
items.summary:
Learn the difference between CLI interactive v. non-interactive modes.
The post GitHub Copilot CLI for Beginners: Interactive v. non-interactive mode appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub Copilot CLI is a new developer tool with distinct interactive vs non‑interactive modes. No existing documentation exists in the wiki, so a dedicated guide is needed.

items.title: GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with Markdown
items.summary:
Discover how to format and edit your comments and posts using Markdown.
The post GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with Markdown appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: A beginner‑level guide to Markdown on GitHub fills a gap for new contributors. The wiki currently lacks a basic Markdown tutorial.

items.title: Securing the git push pipeline: Responding to a critical remote code execution vulnerability
items.summary:
How we validated, fixed, and investigated a critical vulnerability in under two hours, and confirmed no exploitation.
The post Securing the git push pipeline: Responding to a critical remote code execution vulnerability appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: A critical remote code execution vulnerability in the git‑push pipeline is a high‑impact security incident. Detailed coverage is essential for the wiki’s security section.

items.title: An update on GitHub availability
items.summary:
Here’s what we’ve done—and what we’re still doing—to improve our availability and reliability.
The post An update on GitHub availability appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub availability updates are relevant for operations teams monitoring service health. A concise status page fits the wiki’s operational documentation.

items.title: Trip report: Node.js collaboration summit (2026 London)
items.reason: The Node.js collaboration summit report provides community insights and future roadmap hints. Though not deeply technical, it merits a short summary entry.
